title: Information and Consultation of Employees Regulations 2004
chapters: SI 2004/3426. 40 Regulations in 9 Parts. Implements EU Directive 2002/14/EC (Information and Consultation Directive). Amended by Employment Rights (Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations 2019 (SI 2019/731) — reduced employee request threshold from 10% to 2%. The Regulations remain in force as retained EU law post-Brexit.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Trade and Industry under the European Communities Act 1972 s.2(2)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Phased commencement: 150+ employees from 6 April 2005, 100+ from 6 April 2007, 50+ from 6 April 2008. Since 6 April 2008, the Regulations apply to all undertakings with 50 or more employees. The 2019 amendment (reducing the employee request threshold from 10% to 2%) took effect from 6 April 2020.

significantAmendments.effect: Reduced the employee request threshold from 10% to 2% of the total workforce (subject to a minimum of 15 employees). This significantly lowered the barrier for employees to trigger the statutory process — previously, in a 500-employee business, 50 employees were needed; now only 10 are required.

precedingDocuments.title: EU Directive 2002/14/EC (Information and Consultation Directive)
precedingDocuments.significance: The parent Directive establishing minimum requirements for informing and consulting employees in the EU. The UK implemented it through the ICE Regulations 2004. Post-Brexit, the Directive no longer applies to the UK, but the implementing Regulations remain in force as retained EU law.

precedingDocuments.title: Post-Brexit retention
precedingDocuments.significance: The ICE Regulations were retained as domestic law by the EU (Withdrawal) Act 2018. They continue to apply to UK employers with 50+ employees. The Government has not indicated any intention to repeal or replace them.

leadingCases.name: Limited reported case law on ICE Regulations
leadingCases.principle: The ICE Regulations have generated relatively little case law because most disputes are resolved through the Central Arbitration Committee (CAC) process rather than through tribunal or court proceedings. The CAC publishes its decisions but these have limited precedential value. Key areas of interpretation include: the definition of 'undertaking' (separate legal entity, not just a workplace), the meaning of 'employee' for threshold purposes, and the scope of the employer's obligation to provide information.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE — monitor CAC decisions for interpretive developments, particularly on the scope of consultation obligations and the boundary between information and consultation.

parts.title: Part 1 — Scope, Threshold, and Trigger Mechanism

sections.title: Who is covered, how obligations are triggered, and the 2% request threshold
sections.summary: The Regulations apply to 'undertakings' with 50 or more employees. An 'undertaking' is a public or private undertaking carrying out an economic activity — it must be a separate legal entity, not just a workplace or business unit. The employee count is based on the average over the previous 12 months. CRITICALLY: the Regulations do NOT create an automatic obligation to inform and consult. The obligations are TRIGGERED either by: (a) a valid written request from at least 2% of employees (minimum 15, maximum 2,500), or (b) the employer voluntarily initiating the process through formal notification. If no request is made, the status quo continues — the employer has no obligation to set up formal information and consultation arrangements. The 2% threshold (reduced from 10% in April 2020) significantly lowered the barrier for employees to trigger the process.
sections.text:
SCOPE:
(1) Applies to undertakings with 50+ employees in the UK.
(2) 'Undertaking' = a separate legal entity carrying out an economic activity, whether for gain or not. Companies within the same group are treated as separate undertakings.
(3) Employee count = average over the previous 12 months. Agency workers whose contract with the agency is not a contract of employment are treated as having such a contract for counting purposes (Agency Workers Regulations 2010).

TRIGGER MECHANISM:
(1) Employee request: a valid written request must be—
(a) made by at least 2% of employees in the undertaking,
(b) subject to a minimum of 15 employees,
(c) subject to a maximum of 2,500 employees,
(d) in writing, specifying the names of the employees making it and the date sent.
(2) The request can be sent directly to the employer OR through the CAC (which preserves anonymity).
(3) Employer notification: the employer can voluntarily initiate the process.

NO AUTOMATIC OBLIGATION:
If no valid request is made by employees and the employer does not initiate, there is NO obligation to set up formal information and consultation arrangements. Many employers with 50+ employees have never received a request and have no current ICE obligations.

PRE-EXISTING AGREEMENTS:
Where a pre-existing information and consultation agreement already covers all employees (e.g., through a works council or staff forum), a higher threshold applies: 40% of employees must support the request to override the existing arrangement. This protects employers who already have effective consultation mechanisms.
sections.keyPrinciple: The ICE Regulations are a DEMAND-LED framework — obligations arise only when employees demand them. An employer with 50+ employees who has never received a valid request has no current statutory obligation under these Regulations. However, the 2020 reduction of the threshold from 10% to 2% means that requests are now much easier for employees to make. In a 500-employee business, just 10 employees can trigger the entire statutory process.
refs: Agency Workers Regulations 2010 Reg.19 (agency workers counted for ICE threshold)
EU Directive 2002/14/EC (parent Directive — no longer applies post-Brexit but informs interpretation)

regulatoryGuidance.title: The Information and Consultation Regulations — Guide for Employers and Employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The CAC's official guide to the Regulations including the process, timelines, and enforcement.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Informing and consulting employees
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: ACAS guidance on good practice in employee consultation, including the ICE Regulations framework.

commonErrors: Assuming the Regulations create an automatic obligation on all employers with 50+ employees — they do NOT. Obligations are triggered only by a valid employee request or employer-initiated notification
Not monitoring employee counts — if the average headcount over 12 months crosses the 50 threshold, the undertaking falls within scope. Fast-growing businesses should track this

Treating the undertaking as a group rather than a separate legal entity — each legal entity is assessed separately. A group with 1,000 employees across 5 companies, each with fewer than 50, has NO company in scope

Ignoring anonymous employee requests made through the CAC — employees can submit requests via the CAC to preserve anonymity. The employer must respond regardless of whether the request is direct or via the CAC

Assuming that an existing staff forum satisfies the Regulations — a pre-existing agreement is recognised only if it meets specific criteria (covers all employees, is in writing, was approved by the employees). An informal staff forum may not qualify

HR managers not having a response protocol for employee requests — when a valid request is received, the HR manager should: (1) acknowledge receipt immediately, (2) verify the request meets the 2%/15-employee minimum within 1 month, (3) notify senior management and legal advisers, (4) initiate the process for electing negotiating representatives, (5) begin negotiations within 3 months. Having no protocol risks breaching the 3-month deadline

Underestimating the practical impact of the 2020 threshold reduction — in a 200-employee business, the old 10% threshold required 20 employees. The new 2% threshold requires only 4 (but minimum 15 applies, so 15 in practice). In a 1,000-employee business, only 20 employees are needed. HR departments should expect more requests and be prepared

parts.title: Part 2 — The Negotiation Process and Standard Provisions

sections.title: Negotiating an agreement, the standard (fallback) provisions, and what must be covered
sections.summary: Once a valid request is received, the employer must initiate negotiations within 3 months. The employer and elected employee negotiating representatives have 6 months (extendable by agreement) to reach a negotiated agreement on how information and consultation will operate. If no agreement is reached, the statutory STANDARD PROVISIONS automatically apply. The standard provisions require the employer to: (1) inform representatives about the recent and probable development of the undertaking's activities and economic situation, (2) inform AND consult on the situation, structure, and probable development of employment (including any measures to reduce employment threats such as redundancies), and (3) inform AND consult on decisions likely to lead to substantial changes in work organisation or contractual relations. Consultation means the 'exchange of views and establishment of a dialogue' — not merely telling representatives what has already been decided.
sections.text:
NEGOTIATION PROCESS (Regs.8-17):
(1) Within 3 months of a valid request, the employer must initiate negotiations.
(2) The employer must arrange for the election of negotiating representatives.
(3) Negotiations must be conducted with a view to reaching a written agreement.
(4) A negotiated agreement must:
(a) set out the circumstances in which the employer must inform and consult employees,
(b) provide for the appointment or election of information and consultation representatives, or provide for information and consultation directly with employees,
(c) cover all employees in the undertaking.
(5) The agreement must be approved by a majority of negotiating representatives who sign it AND approved in a ballot by at least 50% of those who vote.
(6) If no agreement is reached within 6 months, the standard provisions apply automatically.

STANDARD PROVISIONS (Reg.20):
If the standard provisions apply, the employer must:

(a) INFORM representatives on:
— The recent and probable development of the undertaking's activities and economic situation.

(b) INFORM AND CONSULT on:
— The situation, structure, and probable development of employment, and any measures envisaged to address threats to employment (including potential redundancies — even below the 20-employee threshold for collective redundancy consultation under TULRCA 1992 s.188).

(c) INFORM AND CONSULT on:
— Any decisions likely to lead to substantial changes in work organisation or in contractual relations (including collective redundancies under TULRCA 1992 s.188 and transfers under TUPE 2006).

CONSULTATION means the 'exchange of views and establishment of a dialogue'. The employer must:
— Provide information in sufficient time, fashion, and content to enable representatives to study it and prepare for consultation.
— Consult at the appropriate level of management.
— Give a reasoned response to any opinion expressed by representatives.
— Seek to reach agreement on decisions within the scope of management powers.
sections.keyPrinciple: The standard provisions go BEYOND collective redundancy and TUPE consultation. They require information about the general economic situation of the business and consultation on ANY decisions likely to lead to substantial changes in work organisation or contracts — including restructuring, outsourcing, changes to terms and conditions, technology changes, and site closures. An employer who consults only when TUPE or collective redundancy thresholds are reached may still be in breach of the ICE standard provisions.
refs: ICE Regulations 2004 Regs.8-20 (negotiation and standard provisions)
commonErrors: Treating consultation as merely informing representatives of decisions already taken — consultation requires the 'exchange of views and establishment of a dialogue'. Presenting a fait accompli is not consultation

Not providing information in sufficient time for representatives to study it — the information must be given early enough for representatives to conduct 'an adequate study and where necessary to prepare for consultation'
Failing to give a reasoned response to representatives' opinions — the employer must respond to representatives' views with reasons, even if the employer disagrees

Assuming that ICE consultation only applies to redundancies and transfers — the standard provisions cover ANY decisions likely to lead to substantial changes in work organisation or contracts, which includes restructuring, outsourcing, technology changes, and changes to terms and conditions
Not initiating negotiations within 3 months of a valid request — this is a strict deadline. The employer cannot delay by questioning the validity of the request indefinitely

Conducting 'sham' consultation — presenting decisions already taken to representatives as if consultation were still open. Genuine consultation requires: (a) providing information BEFORE the decision is finalised, (b) allowing sufficient time for study and response, (c) engaging in genuine dialogue, (d) considering and responding to representatives' views with reasons, (e) being willing to modify proposals in light of consultation. A presentation followed by 'any questions?' is NOT consultation

HR managers treating ICE consultation as a box-ticking exercise — the standard provisions require a 'dialogue'. HR should prepare a structured consultation plan: information pack → reading period → meeting → written response to views → follow-up meeting if needed → reasoned decision communicated with explanation of how views were considered

parts.title: Part 3 — Enforcement, Penalties, and Representative Protection

sections.title: CAC enforcement, £75,000 penalty, confidentiality, and representative protections
sections.summary: Enforcement is through the Central Arbitration Committee (CAC), not the employment tribunal. If an employer fails to comply with a negotiated agreement or the standard provisions, representatives (or employees) can complain to the CAC. If the CAC finds the complaint well-founded, it makes a declaration and may order the employer to take specified steps within a specified period. If the employer fails to comply with the CAC's order, the representatives can apply to the Employment Appeal Tribunal (EAT) for a PENALTY NOTICE of up to £75,000, payable to the Secretary of State. The penalty is not compensation for the employees — it is a punitive sanction. Representatives are protected from unfair dismissal and detriment for performing their ICE functions. They have a right to paid time off for their representative duties.
sections.text:
ENFORCEMENT (Reg.22):
(1) Complaint to CAC: representatives or employees can complain about non-compliance.
(2) CAC declaration: if well-founded, the CAC makes a declaration and may order compliance.
(3) Penalty: if the employer fails to comply with the CAC order, the EAT may issue a penalty notice of up to £75,000.
(4) The penalty is payable to the Secretary of State, not to the employees.
(5) The EAT must issue the penalty unless satisfied that the failure is excusable.

CONFIDENTIALITY (Regs.25-26):
(1) The employer may designate information as confidential where disclosure would seriously harm the business.
(2) Representatives must respect confidentiality — breach can be a disciplinary offence (if provided for in the agreement).
(3) If representatives dispute the confidentiality designation, they can apply to the CAC for a ruling.
(4) If the CAC finds the information is not genuinely confidential, it can order disclosure.

REPRESENTATIVE PROTECTIONS (Regs.27-33):
(1) Right to paid time off to perform ICE representative functions.
(2) Right not to be subjected to detriment for performing ICE functions.
(3) Right not to be unfairly dismissed for performing ICE functions — automatically unfair dismissal (no qualifying period).
(4) Complaints about detriment and dismissal go to the employment tribunal (not the CAC).
sections.keyPrinciple: The £75,000 penalty is a significant punitive sanction — but it is only reached after a multi-stage process (complaint → CAC declaration → CAC order → EAT penalty). In practice, most employers settle or comply at the CAC stage. The representative protections are strong and mirror other statutory representative protections (TULRCA elected representatives, H&S representatives) — dismissal of an ICE representative for performing their functions is automatically unfair with no qualifying period.
commonErrors: Assuming there is no financial penalty for non-compliance — the £75,000 maximum penalty is real and enforceable through the EAT

Designating ALL business information as confidential to avoid disclosure — the confidentiality exception applies only where disclosure would 'seriously harm' the business. Overuse of the confidentiality designation may itself be challenged at the CAC
Dismissing or disciplining an ICE representative for performing their representative functions — this is automatically unfair dismissal with no qualifying period
Not allowing paid time off for ICE representatives — the right to reasonable paid time off is statutory and enforceable at the employment tribunal

parts.title: Part 4 — Interaction with Other Consultation Regimes

sections.num: Cross-reference: ICE vs TULRCA vs TUPE
sections.title: How ICE consultation interacts with collective redundancy, TUPE, and trade union consultation
sections.summary: The ICE Regulations exist ALONGSIDE other consultation obligations — they do not replace them. An employer may face MULTIPLE overlapping consultation obligations simultaneously: ICE consultation (on business decisions generally), TULRCA 1992 s.188 collective redundancy consultation (where 20+ redundancies are proposed at one establishment within 90 days), TUPE 2006 Reg.13 consultation (on measures in connection with a relevant transfer), and trade union recognition consultation (where a recognised union has the right to be consulted on specific matters). The key difference: ICE is BROADER in scope but WEAKER in enforcement. TULRCA and TUPE have stronger enforcement (protective awards for collective redundancy, compensation for TUPE failures) but narrower trigger conditions. ICE catches decisions that fall BELOW the collective redundancy threshold or OUTSIDE the TUPE transfer context — restructuring, outsourcing, technology changes, site relocations.
sections.text:
INTERACTION MAP:

(1) ICE Regulations: Covers ALL significant business decisions affecting employment. Triggered by employee request. Enforced through CAC/EAT (£75,000 max). BROADER scope.

(2) TULRCA 1992 s.188: Covers COLLECTIVE REDUNDANCIES (20+ at one establishment within 90 days). Triggered automatically by the proposal. Enforced through protective awards (up to 90 days' pay per employee). STRONGER enforcement, NARROWER scope.

(3) TUPE 2006 Reg.13: Covers MEASURES in connection with a relevant transfer. Triggered automatically by the transfer. Enforced through compensation (up to 13 weeks' pay per employee). STRONGER enforcement, NARROWER scope.

(4) Trade union consultation: Where a union is recognised, specific consultation obligations may exist under the recognition agreement or statute (TULRCA 1992 s.181-185 — disclosure of information for collective bargaining).

CRITICAL POINT: The ICE standard provisions require consultation on decisions likely to lead to substantial changes — even if those decisions do NOT trigger TULRCA collective redundancy thresholds (e.g., 15 redundancies, below the 20 threshold) or TUPE obligations (no relevant transfer).

For employers with active ICE arrangements, this means:
— A restructuring affecting 15 employees must still be subject to ICE consultation even though it falls below the collective redundancy threshold.
— An outsourcing that is not a TUPE transfer must still be subject to ICE consultation if it leads to substantial changes in work organisation.
— A technology change that alters working practices must be subject to ICE consultation even though no specific statute requires it.
sections.keyPrinciple: ICE fills the gaps left by TULRCA and TUPE. It catches the decisions that fall BETWEEN the more specific consultation regimes. An employer who consults only when statutory thresholds are reached may still breach ICE obligations for the decisions that fall below those thresholds.
refs: TUPE 2006 Reg.13 (consultation on measures in connection with transfer)
TULRCA 1992 s.181-185 (disclosure of information for collective bargaining)
ICE Regulations 2004 Reg.20 (standard provisions — broader scope)
commonErrors: Assuming that ICE consultation replaces TULRCA or TUPE consultation — it does NOT. All three regimes operate simultaneously. An employer proposing 20+ redundancies during a business transfer must comply with ICE, TULRCA s.188, AND TUPE Reg.13

Only consulting when TULRCA or TUPE thresholds are met, overlooking ICE obligations — ICE catches decisions below those thresholds, including small-scale redundancies, non-TUPE outsourcing, and technology-driven changes to work organisation

Not recognising that ICE consultation covers the ECONOMIC SITUATION of the business generally — the standard provisions require information about the recent and probable development of the undertaking's activities, which goes beyond specific employment decisions

pendingAmendments.instrument: Post-Brexit review of retained EU employment law
pendingAmendments.status: The ICE Regulations are retained EU law. The Government has not announced plans to repeal or amend them. Monitor for any review as part of the broader retained EU law reform programme.
The reduction of the employee request threshold from 10% to 2% (April 2020) was expected to increase the number of requests. Monitor CAC data for trends in request volumes.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — employers with 50+ employees are more likely to receive requests
